SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states its main technical idea (Sec. 3) and formalizes it with straightforward equations for Gram matrices (Eq. 1), transformed Gramians (Eq. 3) and the cross-correlation loss (Eq. 4). Empirical support is provided across several tasks (texture synthesis, inpainting, season transfer) and comparisons to the Gatys et al. baseline are reported (Sec. 4, Figures cited). However, the evaluation is purely qualitative (no quantitative metrics or statistical tests) and several claims (e.g. that added terms "do not hurt the diversity of generated textures") are only empirically asserted without rigorous analysis; the choice of δ is heuristic and not automatically justified.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The core contribution — computing Gram matrices between spatially transformed feature maps to capture long-range spatial co-occurrences — is a clear, concrete extension of Gatys et al. and is presented as a novel modification in Sec. 3. The paper situates the idea relative to prior concepts (relating transformed Gramians to GLCMs) and explores other transformations (flips) in Sec. 4.5, indicating non-trivial methodological innovation rather than mere reapplication of existing methods.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized in a conventional and logical manner (Intro → method (Sec. 2–3) → experiments (Sec. 4) → conclusion (Sec. 5)) and defines notation for Gramians and transformed Gramians (Eqs. 1,3). The δ-selection guideline and implementation details are clearly discussed (Sec. 4.1), and examples/figures are referenced throughout to support claims. There are some presentation issues in the provided text (repeated paragraphs and minor formatting glitches) but the technical exposition and notation remain largely consistent and comprehensible.
